html:
"No one can change the words of Allah" — yet tahrif is the central Muslim claim Logical Inconsistency Jesus / Christology Strong Quran 6:115, 10:64, 18:27
"And the word of your Lord has been fulfilled in truth and in justice. None can alter His words..." (6:115)
"...no change is there in the words of Allah." (10:64)

What the verses say

The Quran repeatedly and emphatically states that no one can alter the words of Allah. This is presented as proof of divine reliability.

Why this is a problem

The standard Islamic explanation for why the Bible contradicts the Quran is tahrif — Jews and Christians corrupted their scriptures. But the Torah and Gospel, per the Quran itself (5:43–48, 3:3), were words revealed by Allah. If "no one can change the words of Allah," then the Bible cannot have been corrupted. And if the Bible was corrupted, then humans did change the words of Allah — falsifying the Quran's own claim.

Islam has held both positions for 1,400 years. The Muslim cannot claim both: "Allah's words are unchangeable" and "the Bible is corrupted." Each rescue attempt weakens the original claim further.

The Muslim response

Tahrif means distortion of meaning or selective interpretation, not physical textual alteration. The core divine message was preserved; what was corrupted was the community's relationship to it and understanding of it.

Why it fails

If tahrif is distortion of meaning rather than text, then the physical words of the Bible are Allah's unchanged words — including the crucifixion, the Trinity, and the sonship of Jesus. The Quran's contradictions of those texts then become contradictions of Allah's own preserved words. The meaning-only interpretation shifts the problem without solving it. 6:115 and 10:64 state plainly that "none can alter" Allah's words — not "none can alter the words but anyone can distort their meaning." The plain reading is about textual integrity, and the apologetic re-definition of tahrif is a post-hoc rescue with no Quranic warrant.

Skins roasted and replaced — eternal torture engineered for maximum pain Logical Inconsistency Treatment of Disbelievers Strong Quran 4:56
"Indeed, those who disbelieve in Our verses — We will drive them into a fire. Every time their skins are roasted through We will replace them with other skins so they may taste the punishment. Indeed, Allah is ever Exalted in Might and Wise."

What the verse says

Allah burns disbelievers eternally. When their skin is destroyed and nerve endings can no longer feel pain, He replaces the skin with fresh skin — so pain resumes at full intensity. This cycle never ends.

Why this is a problem

This is a mechanical description of how Allah engineers maximum suffering. The verse specifically highlights skin-replacement as the solution to a pain-tolerance problem — a design feature to defeat the natural mercy of nerve damage.

Three linked objections: First, a finite creature cannot commit infinite wrong — a 70-year life of unbelief cannot morally warrant unending torture. Second, the verse shows Allah anticipating that normal burning would eventually numb sufferers and correcting for that — this is not impersonal justice but sadistic redesign. Third, every moral tradition that has reflected seriously on punishment recognizes that even murderers do not deserve endless torture. The Quran endorses exactly what moral intuition across cultures rejects.

The Muslim response

Standard replies: Allah is just; disbelievers chose this fate freely; Hell is proportional to the gravity of rejecting the ultimate truth. Alternatively, Hell may be understood metaphorically rather than as a physical description.

Why it fails

The "choice" involved is rejecting a specific Arabic revelation delivered in the 7th century — one billions of humans either never heard, heard in distorted form, or had prior rational grounds to regard as unconvincing. Punishing them eternally for this is not justice; it is rigged justice. On the metaphorical reading: the hadith corpus spends enormous detail on the physical torments of Hell, and the mainstream Sunni position has never been metaphorical. Softening the verse requires abandoning fourteen centuries of traditional reading.

Good from Allah, evil from yourself — direct contradiction two verses apart Contradiction Logical Inconsistency Strong Quran 4:78 vs 4:79
"...Say, 'All [things] are from Allah.'" (4:78)
"What comes to you of good is from Allah, but what comes to you of evil, [O man], is from yourself..." (4:79)

What the verses say

Verse 78: whatever happens — good or evil — is from Allah. Verse 79, the immediately following verse: good is from Allah; evil is from yourself.

Why this is a problem

This is one of the clearest textual contradictions in the Quran. The two verses are adjacent, use the same vocabulary, address the same question (where does evil come from?), and give opposite answers. The problem is amplified by the Quran's own self-test in 4:82: "If it had been from any other than Allah, they would have found within it much contradiction" — a verse demanding contradiction-free scripture located just a few lines above two verses that directly contradict each other.

The Muslim response

The classical Ash'arite harmonization: Allah creates (khalq) all events, but humans acquire (kasb) moral responsibility. This distinction resolves the apparent conflict — both verses are true at different levels.

Why it fails

The creation/acquisition distinction does not appear in either verse — it was invented by theologians centuries later specifically to manage this problem. 4:78 says evil is "from Allah"; 4:79 says it is "from yourself." The attempt to separate the two verses by category — physical events vs. moral failures — also fails: both use the general word sayyi'ah (bad thing/misfortune) and the context is about events happening to people, not distinguishing cosmic events from moral decisions. The verses contradict each other, and the self-test in 4:82 is one of the casualties.

"You will never be able to be just between wives" — yet polygamy stays authorized Contradiction Women Strong Quran 4:3 vs 4:129
"...marry those that please you of [other] women, two or three or four. But if you fear that you will not be just, then [marry only] one..." (4:3)
"And you will never be able to be equal [in feeling] between wives, even if you should strive [to do so]..." (4:129)

What the verses say

4:3 permits polygamy up to four wives — conditional on the husband being just among them. 4:129 declares flatly that a man will never be able to be just between wives, no matter how hard he tries.

Why this is a problem

The condition for permission (4:3) is declared impossible in (4:129). If justice between wives is the prerequisite, and justice cannot be achieved, then polygamy cannot validly be practiced. Yet it remains lawful across the Islamic world because Muslims treat 4:3 as active. Either reading is uncomfortable for orthodoxy: take 4:129 at face value and polygamy is functionally forbidden; take 4:3 at face value and 4:129 becomes mere rhetorical hyperbole.

The Muslim response

The standard distinction: 4:3 is about material justice (equal nights, equal financial support), while 4:129 is about emotional justice — acknowledging that a man cannot help loving one wife more. The two verses operate in different domains.

Why it fails

The distinction is interpretively possible but textually invented — neither verse draws it. A book that claims to be "clear" (11:1, 16:89) should not require imported theological scaffolding to avoid contradicting itself within the same surah. The more honest reading is that 4:129 concedes what 4:3 demanded: perfect justice between wives is not humanly achievable — which means the license to marry four was never realistically conditional on a condition no one can meet. The Quran's own "self-test" verse (4:82) is implicated here too.

Amputation, crucifixion, or exile — the penalty for "waging war against Allah" Treatment of Disbelievers Women Strong Quran 5:33–34
"Indeed, the penalty for those who wage war against Allah and His Messenger and strive upon earth [to cause] corruption is none but that they be killed or crucified or that their hands and feet be cut off from opposite sides or that they be exiled from the land..."

What the verse says

For "waging war against Allah and His Messenger" and "corruption on earth," the Quran prescribes a menu: execution, crucifixion, alternating-sides amputation, or exile.

Why this is a problem

  1. The crimes are undefined. "Waging war against Allah" and "corruption on earth" are not specified. Classical jurists stretched them to cover highway robbery, apostasy, heresy, armed rebellion, unauthorized religious expression, and in modern states, drug trafficking and dissent. A law whose triggers are limitless is a law of whoever holds the sword.
  2. The punishments are theatrical. Crucifixion as a specific prescribed method is public-display punishment designed to terrify populations. Alternating-sides amputation (right hand + left foot) is the same: designed for maximal visible horror, not proportional justice.

ISIS cited 5:33 as its legal basis for public crucifixions and hand-foot amputations in Syria and Iraq between 2014 and 2019. They did not need to stretch the verse — the plain reading supports exactly what they did.

The Muslim response

This verse was revealed in the specific context of the Urayna tribe who converted, committed murder, stole camels, and fled. The penalty was for that specific crime. Classical jurisprudence built procedural restrictions requiring specific conditions before any penalty applies.

Why it fails

The verse explicitly addresses a general category — "those who wage war against Allah and His Messenger" — not just the Urayna. Classical Muslim legal scholarship treated it as general legislation for all time, which is why it appears in Sharia codes across multiple countries today. "It was originally specific" does not change how the tradition has read, codified, and applied it for 1,400 years.

The Night Journey — Muhammad flies to Jerusalem on a winged creature Strange / Obscure Prophetic Character Strong Quran 17:1 (with hadith Bukhari 3887 and Muslim 162)
"Exalted is He who took His Servant by night from al-Masjid al-Haram to al-Masjid al-Aqsa, whose surroundings We have blessed, to show him of Our signs..."

What the verse says

In a single night, Allah transports Muhammad from Mecca to "al-Masjid al-Aqsa." The hadith tradition elaborates that Muhammad rode a winged creature called Buraq, met earlier prophets, ascended through seven heavens, and bargained with Moses over the number of daily prayers (negotiated from 50 down to 5).

Why this is a problem

  1. There was no Al-Aqsa Mosque in 621 CE. The current Al-Aqsa Mosque was built in 705 CE — 73 years after Muhammad's death and over 80 years after the Night Journey. The Temple Mount in 621 CE held ruins of the Jewish Temple, not a mosque.
  2. The elaborate story (Buraq, seven heavens, prayer negotiation) is not in the Quran. It rests entirely on hadith collected two to three centuries after the event. The Quran says only that Allah took His servant from one mosque to another.
  3. Muhammad's own contemporaries reacted with disbelief. Many Meccan Muslims apostatized when they heard the claim (Bukhari 3886, Ibn Ishaq's sira). Abu Bakr earned the title "al-Siddiq" (the faithful) specifically for accepting the story without verification — the honorific itself signals the story demanded extraordinary trust.

The Muslim response

"Al-Masjid al-Aqsa" means "the farthest place of prostration" — not necessarily a building. The journey was to the Temple Mount site, not to a structure that didn't exist yet. Alternatively, the journey was spiritual, not physical — a vision Muhammad received.

Why it fails

Mainstream Islamic tradition — including the accepted sahih hadiths — pins the destination to a specific physical location in Jerusalem and treats the site as the foundation of Islam's claim on Jerusalem. The non-specific reading undermines that entire claim. The "spiritual vision" reading was held by some early scholars (including Aisha per one hadith) but rejected by the majority, and retreating to it now to avoid the historical problem is modern apologetics against the classical doctrine. The night-prayer-bargaining story (Moses tells Muhammad to keep going back and negotiate down from 50 prayers) is particularly difficult to read as spiritually edifying.

"Strike their necks" — the beheading command Treatment of Disbelievers Strong Quran 47:4 (also 8:12)
"So when you meet those who disbelieve [in battle], strike [their] necks until, when you have inflicted slaughter upon them, then secure their bonds, and either [confer] favor afterwards or ransom [them] until the war lays down its burdens..."

What the verse says

When Muslims meet disbelievers in battle: behead until you have inflicted sufficient slaughter, then and only then take captives. Survivors may be freed or ransomed.

Why this is a problem

The verse does not say "defeat them in battle" — it specifies the technique ("strike the necks") and sets a killing threshold before captive-taking begins. The priority is killing first, captive-taking second — offensive choreography, not defensive framing.

Modern jihadist movements cite 47:4 directly and do not need to distort the text. The verse historically provided scriptural warrant for ritualized beheading practices from the early caliphates through Saudi judicial beheadings and ISIS execution videos. Classical Islamic military law developed the verse into operational principles, treating it as standing legislation.

The Muslim response

"Strike the necks" is idiomatic Arabic for "kill in battle," not a specific instruction to behead. The verse governs battlefield conduct only and does not authorize civilian beheadings.

Why it fails

The idiom itself is violent and specific, and the classical tradition has read and applied it literally in military contexts. The "battlefield only" defense is acknowledged as technically correct — 47:4 does concern combat — but it establishes that beheading is the prescribed method and that killing precedes capture. In asymmetric conflict where the "battlefield" is diffuse, the verse supplies both the method and the priority ordering. Limiting it to Muhammad's specific wars is a modern apologetic move against the classical reading that treated it as permanent Islamic war law.

"Do not take the Jews and Christians as allies" Treatment of Disbelievers Strong Quran 5:51
"O you who have believed, do not take the Jews and the Christians as allies... And whoever is an ally to them among you — then indeed, he is [one] of them."

What the verse says

Muslims are forbidden from taking Jews and Christians as allies (awliya'). Taking them as allies makes one of them.

Why this is a problem

Awliya' in classical Arabic covers a broad range: alliance, friendship, protection, trust, patronage. The verse names two specific religious communities as off-limits. Classical tafsir (Tabari, Ibn Kathir) read the prohibition broadly, and modern conservative Muslim discourse continues to apply it to personal interfaith friendship. A religion that forbids friendship across religious lines at the scriptural level has baked inter-communal hostility into its foundation.

The Muslim response

Classical apologetics narrows awliya' to military-political alliance: 5:51 prohibits formal alliance with hostile Jewish and Christian powers in a wartime context, not personal relationships with individual Jews and Christians. Modern reformists cite classical exceptions permitting peaceful coexistence and interfaith friendship.

Why it fails

The narrow-military reading is the modern apologetic move that reformists use to make Islam compatible with pluralistic societies — welcome reform that requires reading against the classical grain. Classical tafsir applied the prohibition broadly to personal as well as political relationships, and modern conservative discourse follows that reading. A religion whose founding scripture prohibits religious-category alliance (even narrowly) has embedded identity politics into its ethical framework at the textual level.

Allah asks Jesus: "Did you tell people to take you and your mother as gods?" Jesus / Christology Contradiction Strong Quran 5:116
"O Jesus, Son of Mary, did you say to the people, 'Take me and my mother as deities besides Allah?'"

What the verse says

Allah confronts Jesus about whether he instructed people to worship him and Mary as two gods alongside Allah. Jesus denies this.

Why this is a problem

The Christian Trinity is Father, Son, and Holy Spirit — not Father, Mary, and Jesus. No Christian sect has ever defined the Trinity as including Mary. The verse misrepresents the doctrine it is attempting to refute. A divine text correcting Christian theology should be engaging the Christianity Christians actually confess, not a different three-deity schema that no Christian has held.

The Muslim response

5:116 may address Collyridian-style sects or functional-veneration practices rather than misidentifying the Trinity. The "take me and my mother as deities" phrasing could target popular devotional practice that effectively treated Mary as divine, regardless of official doctrine.

Why it fails

The Collyridian hypothesis rests on a sect attested only in Epiphanius's Panarion and never evidenced as widespread or representative. Orthodox Christianity — Catholic, Protestant, Eastern Orthodox — has never defined the Trinity as Father/Mary/Jesus. If the Quran were addressing "functional" rather than official theology, the text should say so. A divine author correcting Christian doctrine is accountable to the doctrine Christians actually hold. The Quran substitutes Mary for the Holy Spirit and presents the substitution as the target doctrine — which is a factual error about Christian belief.

Jesus prophesied "Ahmad" — no Gospel records this Jesus / Christology Strong Quran 61:6
"Jesus said: 'I am the messenger of Allah to you... bringing good tidings of a messenger to come after me, whose name is Ahmad.'"

What the verse says

Jesus foretells a prophet named "Ahmad" (identified with Muhammad) who will come after him.

Why this is a problem

No Gospel contains this prophecy. No early Christian document records Jesus promising a future prophet named Ahmad or any Arabic equivalent. The only evidence for this prophecy is Islam's own text — a circularly-attested claim with no independent corroboration.

The Muslim response

Classical apologetics links the "Ahmad" prophecy to John 14:16's "Paraclete" (Parakletos). Some apologists argue that Parakletos ("comforter") is a mistranscription of Periklytos ("praised," analogous to Arabic Ahmad). Early Christian manuscript corruption obscured the original prophecy.

Why it fails

No Greek manuscript evidence supports Periklytos anywhere in early copies of John 14. Every surviving early Greek manuscript reads Parakletos. The early church fathers consistently identified the Paraclete as the Holy Spirit, already sent at Pentecost (Acts 2). The mistranscription theory requires a corruption so ancient and comprehensive that no pre-corruption manuscript survives anywhere in the Christian world — a claim with zero independent evidence. A prophecy whose textual support requires a conjectured mis-spelling unattested in any manuscript is not prediction; it is retroactive construction.

"Stay in your houses and do not display yourselves" Women Strong Quran 33:33
"Abide in your houses and do not display yourselves as [was] the display of the former times of ignorance."

What the verse says

Originally addressed to the Prophet's wives; extended by classical tradition to all Muslim women as textual warrant for public-space restrictions.

Why this is a problem

Classical jurisprudence across all four Sunni schools consistently extended the principle beyond Muhammad's wives to all Muslim women, treating 33:33 as the textual foundation for female public-life restrictions. Modern Saudi-style confinement, Taliban-era Afghan home-confinement policies, and Iranian public-attire laws all cite this verse as warrant. The verse's framing — "former times of ignorance" — presupposes that pre-Islamic Arabian female public life was degraded, advancing a specific social model that has constrained women across fourteen centuries.

The Muslim response

Classical apologetics limits 33:33 to Muhammad's wives (Ummahat al-Mu'minin), who had distinctive public-religious roles warranting specific conduct guidelines. Modern apologists emphasize that broader female public-life restrictions extracted from this verse are misapplications, not the text's intent.

Why it fails

The "only Muhammad's wives" narrowing is modern reformist work against the classical grain. The same Sunni legal schools that produced Islamic jurisprudence universally extended the verse's principle, and states implementing Sharia today cite it as standing divine instruction. The "former times of ignorance" framing is itself the problem — it frames female public presence as a pre-Islamic defect to be corrected, embedding gender restriction as the Islamic ideal regardless of how narrowly one reads the original address.

Allah threatens Muhammad's wives with replacement — including virgins Women Strong Quran 66:5
"Perhaps his Lord, if he divorced you, would substitute for him wives better than you — submitting, believing... previously married and virgins."

What the verse says

After the Mariyah/honey dispute, Allah warns Muhammad's wives: comply or Allah will provide better replacements, specifying both previously married women and virgins.

Why this is a problem

This verse is the culmination of the Mariyah scandal (66:1–5): Muhammad's wives were upset that he was having intimate relations with a slave concubine; a divine revelation arrived rebuking them for objecting and threatening them with a divine replacement supply including virgins. The trigger is a domestic complaint about a concubine. The resolution is Allah threatening the complainants on the Prophet's behalf.

Aisha's documented observation — "I see your Lord hastens to fulfill your desires" — captures the structural problem: revelations concerning the Prophet's domestic arrangements consistently resolve in his favor. The verse gives no weight to the wives' perspective. It treats divine threat of replacement as the appropriate response to women voicing domestic grievances.

The Muslim response

The verse addresses the wives' deviation from their special responsibilities as "Mothers of the Believers." Their behavior (gossip, conspiracy, pressuring the Prophet) was a serious breach of their unique roles, warranting divine intervention. The threat is instructive, not vindictive.

Why it fails

The "special responsibilities" framing does not remove what the verse does: it deploys divine authority to threaten women who objected to their husband sleeping with a slave in their shared domestic space. The pattern across 33:37 (Zaynab), 33:50 (special marriage permissions), and 66:1–5 (Mariyah) is consistent — each time a personal situation favors Muhammad, a revelation arrives to validate it. Aisha's inside-the-household observation about the pattern is the most honest commentary the tradition has produced on these verses.
